speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number 2 with survivor David Klebanow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Dr. Klebanow, let's continue. Tell me about your experience in the Polish Army.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the Polish army, I will say it was not a bad experience. Of course, we was in-- living with the Polish young doctors who graduated, so all doctors. Or not only doctor, there also were the-- who started pharmacy. So it was-- and they studied dentistry together. Everything was together.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who did you practice medicine on during that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't. I started right after I graduated. I was working at the Jewish hospital in Bialystok. It was a very fine Jewish hospital. And the-- but I was mobilized. And I had to leave. And the-- and the--

speaker: interviewer
text: While you were in the army itself, did you-- did you have patients?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't have any patients. I was just dressed in military stock that is a simple soldier. And after half a year, I advanced to a-- to a-- now, what is the lowest, lowest non-commissioned officer, you know, not, not sergeant but--

speaker: interviewer
text: Colonel, lieutenant--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, lieutenant is officer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Corporal, I don't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Doesn't matter.

speaker: interviewee
text: Doesn't matter, you know. It is-- it is a--

speaker: interviewer
text: You were not a general. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not a general. With this, I helped there and helped. And I was sent to a regiment to be a doctor in a regiment.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Bialystok.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I lived there. I--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you live?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the-- where the regiment was located.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you were not able to live with your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: I could live-- no, not, not with-- I had to live in the regiment. I can go out. I can spend time with my parents. They knew where I am. But basically, I had to live this life here in this regiment. I had a room for myself. And I had a helper for myself, you know. It was really better conditions. And the-- and the-- but I had to live there.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you remain in that position?

speaker: interviewee
text: Six months.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I finally, you know, I advanced to a sergeant. Meanwhile, in the six years--

speaker: interviewer
text: Six months.

speaker: interviewee
text: --in the in the-- when I finished, I was told that we are applying for you to become an officer in the Polish Army. That is usually-- it was unusual thing. Normally, they would call for another six weeks as a sergeant. And then my-- apparently I behaved as a-- in the military conditions very well. And I knew that I [? behaved ?] behaved very well. And the-- and the, the colonel, the head of the regiment, applied to-- for me to become a full Polish officer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, he applied. It was automatic already.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then in a year or two years, I was called for four weeks. And already, I was dressed in the-- came dressed as a officer. I knew I had to, to dress in an officer in high boots. And the-- and the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was already-- it was already an advance.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was year, I think 1935. So, so no, we were-- I will receive-- better '36.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had you heard about Hitler yet?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I already heard about Hitler, yeah, at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you concerned? You are already a man--

speaker: interviewee
text: Very much concerned.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever think, perhaps, at that point, to try to leave Poland to go to--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I was-- when I was taking-- when I was still in medical school, and I taking my final in internal medicine, was a very strict professor and quite anti-Semitic, known anti-Semitic. And we are faring very well-- I swear, we were three, three, together with two my friends. And the-- especially, likely he asked me some questions, you know, where normally, I wouldn't know. But I was getting from my other friend, the And I was reading. I couldn't speak. But I knew how to read and how to understand. And I came some subjects-- medical subjects, just what he asked me. And I very quietly answered. And he was just surprised that I could answer. All right, I fared very well in the other questions. But then he was very much in the-- involved in the-- in the-- how it is-- how to-- [NON-ENGLISH], how to-- in spas.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Spas. You know what is spa?

speaker: interviewer
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: Spa, health spa.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Health spas, he was very promoting, saying that this is a very good [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] for health. And he had lectures on these spas. And the-- spas. And here, he, he goes now into the spas. And the first question you hear is about German spas.

speaker: interviewer
text: What does that mean to you? How did--

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't answer this question.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why not?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because at that time, I couldn't stand Hitler was in the power. It is Hitler was already entered into the picture, anti-Semitic. And I don't want to know any German spas. He looked at me. Then he asked me about from Yugoslavia-- spas.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of spas were these? These were-- were these places people would-- you would-- a doctor would send people to?

speaker: interviewee
text: My deary-- my dear, spas for rheumatic--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: --for rheumatics conditions, for heart conditions.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, I understand.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it was medical indications for the-- for-- to-- to send to such places.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he asked me first about German spas. And I didn't answer. He look at me. And he asked me about Yugoslavia. And I gave him a full answer. He looked at me. And then he started to ask me about Polish spas. I answered him full. He understood that I just didn't want to talk about the Germans because it was Hitler, anti-Semitism, et cetera.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you feel endangered in any way in 1936 as a Jew?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1936 I was not-- I didn't feel anything. At that time, I already-- no, at that time, I was still in, in Jewish hospital in [NON-ENGLISH] making my residency, residency. And in '37, with 29, 30 years of age, I got a position of a chief in obstetrics and gynecology in a rather small city called Miedzyrzec-- in Jewish, Mezritch. And, and that is no-- it was a big, big for, for, for, for us-- for, for us in, in the America. And the American Jews gave money. And they build a beautiful hundred-bed hospital in--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mezritch?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Mezritch. And the-- there was a--

speaker: interviewer
text: Why'd they give money for a hospital in such a small place?

speaker: interviewee
text: It is-- oh, it was 118,000 Jews for this there. It is very Jewish city. Mezrich was very Jewish. There-- with a special industry in the-- in the-- in the-- oh, it was a-- they were the, the only who, who did it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did you-- how long did you remain in Mezrich?

speaker: interviewee
text: Up to 1st of September, '39.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was the first day of the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was right away called into the army. And on the 1st of September, I was already wearing the-- I was an officer, doctor in the Polish Army. Two days later, I was sent to the-- to the front.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not-- to the west.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you end up? In what city?

speaker: interviewee
text: We, we didn't-- when they, they-- we waited for catastrophe, you know. We went, you know-- I was a doctor attached to a you call the-- we call it the division. But you guys say battalion of artillery, just artillery.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you had specialized in obstetrics and gynecology.

speaker: interviewee
text: Just disregard. I was also trained as a-- as a doctor-- as a doctor-- military doctor, and a military doctor on their outpost. That is the-- it is basically evaluate, can you keep? To send, where to send, to, to field hospital, maybe to, to field hospital? I-- I decided what to do-- what to do with this-- with this--

speaker: interviewer
text: Like emergency medicine?

speaker: interviewee
text: Emergency medicine, correct.

speaker: interviewer
text: So where did-- I want to ask you before you continue, though. Here you were man of 30, what about your romantic life?

speaker: interviewee
text: Romantic life at that time-- at 30, it was basically better to say 30 years, 32, 31. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] A friend who was with me in medical school-- he was one year younger, Dereczinski-- asked me to come to Vienna. He want to introduce me to his sister. She was already a doctor, graduated recently, recently in Italy. And I came. And I was introduced. And I like her very much. And probably should she liked me also very much. And we-- sure, I was in Mezritch. She is in Vilna. And the-- I couldn't come right away again. But shortly thereafter, you know, I came again to see her. It is not to like now, where you take a flight, you know. You have to go by train, you know. It is a-- it is a complicated, complicated things.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the woman's first name?

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her first name?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: [NON-ENGLISH]? No--

speaker: interviewee
text: I am sorry, Sacha.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sacha.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was Sacha, the Jewish Sarah, Sarah. He's-- she was also from a well-to-do family. Shortly, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we were already thinking about getting married. Here the war broke work out, September '39. Here, 10 days later, I am a prisoner of war with the Germans. Here I am staying with the Germans. They were very good to me at this-- that time. They sent me to the hospital. They said there are many soldiers, wounded soldiers, but hardly doctor is there. I came, you know, really, it was one or two doctors. And they sent, you know, more doctors. But at the beginning, was short. It was very nice when I, I was-- so they caught me in a village.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Germans caught you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And the first question was, yeah, I told him, in German, you know, I told him, I said, I am a doctor. And the first question was, [SPEAKING GERMAN]? And are you a Jew? And I thought you know, well, that's my last minute, you know, of my life. No, he was very gentle.

speaker: interviewer
text: You told him yes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, sure, you know, I told him, I am a-- jawohl. You know, sure, you're military? Jawohl. And he was very fine. And he brought me-- brought me-- he came with a Volkswagen. And then on the dusty road, he asked me to go into the Volkswagen. The [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] soldiers, what, what they did, you know, with-- who with me [NON-ENGLISH] people.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is that?

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: I don't understand what that is.

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH] ?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, you know the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Sanitation?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it's not sanitation. [NON-ENGLISH], you know, in the sense they were assigned to my-- as my unit. They were trained how to make, make a, a-- change-- change a dressing, how to put on a stretcher, how to, to, to, to transport such and all. It was--

speaker: interviewer
text: I understand.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your assistants.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they-- assistants, you can say so. And the, the car didn't move. So I wanted, you know, to come out. He said, [? nein ?], you stay here. And then my two soldiers had to push-- to push this car until it came out of trouble. And he brought me to a city called Siedlce.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of place was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: It is-- it is-- it is-- it was quite a nice city in-- in the-- in the eastern part of Germany. It is east of Warsaw. And then-- and he brought me to-- brought me to a big, open space. And I see there's a group of 30, 40 German officers, officers. Some are officers. Some are guests in SS uniforms. I understand the day I see from the sign that they are all doctors or in, in the-- in the-- in the-- in the-- in health department. And I came out of the wagon, and I saluted them. And all 40, either by salute or by stretching the hand-- although he before told them that, that I am a Jew-- everybody saluted. That was the attitude in '39. In came a major and said, please--

speaker: interviewer
text: Because you were all doctors?

speaker: interviewee
text: A doctor, you know, maybe, you know, maybe the, the-- yes, for sure, you know, it was some, some respect in this-- in-- from this point of view. And they-- I-- with the same Volkswagen I was brought to the hospital in Siedlce. And I was told, you have to stay there. Stay there. And I stayed there. I got my, my small room. And I started to work with wounded soldiers.

speaker: interviewer
text: These were German soldiers?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, Polish soldiers. Germans, they were not allowed. It was only Polish soldiers. It was in the city hospital of Siedlce. Shortly, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] there was-- Siedlce was, at the beginning, was occupied by the Germans. Then, you know, somehow on agreement with Russia, Siedlce has to belong to the Russians. So suddenly, the Germans moving out. And they say to all doctors, personnel who wants to go to the west can go with us to the west. And who wants to stay, will stay. I stayed. And here the Russians came. But after four days, was already again a change. The, the Germans are coming again to Siedlce. So there was a question of whether River Bug or River Wisla is a border. It is-- they didn't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said, [? because I informed ?] the-- they are coming. I said, went to the director, I say, please, we have-- there were several hundred wounded. From this several hundred, now we have around 30, 40, 40-- we-- enough still doctors. Can I leave?

speaker: interviewee
text: And he say, absolutely. You can leave. And he allowed me to leave and to go to Bialystok. Bialystok was in the Russian hands. So I went to Bialystok, Bialystok. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was there? Why did you go--

speaker: interviewee
text: My, my, my my, parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh-huh.

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents were there. And the-- I stayed in, in Bialystok. I was little afraid, you know. I was all the time a Zionist. I was-- belonged to Zionist's organization in the university. I was very active summertime in the student's organization in Bialystok as a-- representing the Zionist people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And now comes the communists. And now the leftist students who have a, a, a-- but it was--

speaker: interviewer
text: What would they have?

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: What would the leftist students have?

speaker: interviewee
text: Students? They're very, you know-- they're very with the Russians, you know. They are all the communists, you know. So they got positions right away, et cetera. But, in general, they didn't do me any, any harm. I--

speaker: interviewer
text: If you were a Zionist--

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: If you were a Zionist--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --and a leader of Zionist young people--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why didn't you think of going to Palestine?

speaker: interviewee
text: I will tell you the simple thing why. The, the man who was the head of the remaining-- all the doctor.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't recall his name.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: He is in Bialystok. He works in the-- in the administration-- health administration.

speaker: interviewer
text: Of the Jewish hospital?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the Jewish-- no, it was in the health administration of the city. There was an episode. When I was in high school in Miedzyrzec-- Mezritch-- there was two other Jews, one Grinburg and one Kannel. Kannel went to study medicine, I know, somewhere in Czechoslovakia. He was not admitted to-- Grinburg went studying in Vilna-- I don't recall what. But he got involved, became a communist. And he was distributing on the street literature.

speaker: interviewer
text: Literature.

speaker: interviewee
text: And here, you know, Russian-- Polish police noticed him and started to chase. And the place where he could find it was just-- I am living not far away. He came, ran to me. And I was-- ran to me. And he tells me, I am running away from police. He is my friend. I worked with him. I lived with him, work with him two years, you know, in, in, in duration. It was-- I, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I say, you stay in my room. I went to the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] where I could meet the leftist leaders over the Jewish students. And I went there. And I said, please, Grinburg is in my apartment. They knew already everything. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They said-- they said, please be patient. We will take him away when at the darkness. Fine. And really, when it became dark, they came. They picked him up. And they took him away. He disappeared. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But it was, nevertheless-- I put my whole-- my whole career into danger. If the Pole-- Poles followed him correctly and found him, I would be-- and I was keeping him-- I would be responsible.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that this people didn't forget. And they didn't forget. And they knew about this, when I didn't forget. It was-- that was all right. It was-- hold on, approximately 19-- 20-- 20-- 8-- '29. And, and that is what? Now it is 1939-- 10 years. It's the-- you cannot forget.

speaker: interviewee
text: I know that he was sent to Russia. He didn't want, you know, to appear. He would be arrested, arrested, to go for four or five years into prison. And somehow, he-- they sent him to Russia. What happened to him, I don't know. But these men who managed all these things with him, who knew that I am keeping in my apartment was this same man, you know-- now in the administration. So from one side, I was for him a Zionist. And from the other side, I am the man who helped his, his, his-- and he just didn't make any, any, any, any, any noise, we'll say so.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't remember his name.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, tell me what happened in 1939, then, after the war--

speaker: interviewer
text: --what did you do in Bialystok?

speaker: interviewee
text: --after this, oh, I was prisoner of war. The Germans were-- again. I had to come back, I left, you know, Siedlce. And I am now in Bialystok. And in Bialystok, shortly, I get a position in a-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in a-- hospital only for obstetrics. A small Jewish-- that's what we were, three doctors.

speaker: interviewer
text: In, In, in, in the city of Bialystok?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the city of Bialystok.

speaker: interviewer
text: By the way, did you marry this woman in the meantime?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now I-- at that time, Vilna becomes Lithuania. And the, the border is closed. I even cannot write letters. We are completely, completely--

speaker: interviewer
text: Separated.

speaker: interviewee
text: --separated. And only-- that was in 1939, I could-- I got information she's now not in Vilna. She's in Kaunas. Kaunas is another city. And her, her brother, who is a doctor, came to me again and said, now time to go-- to go there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was in June, and we-- in June of '41. And we had to cross the, the border. But it was not really guarded border between Russia and, and Lithuania. And we-- I went, you know, to Kaunas. And I met her. And we decided there is no, no time to lose. We will get married. Her father has to flee Vilna. He was a rich man. I may-- we went to see him. He was living in a small city. And we came back. And we said, we'll go. We'll get married.

speaker: interviewer
text: So where did you get married?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Kaunas.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who performed the ceremony?

speaker: interviewee
text: A rabbi. And who was the rabbi, I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was that? Did you get married in a synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Kaunas.

speaker: interviewer
text: In a synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was not in the synagogue. I don't recall where, where it was. I wanted not in a synagogue.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you bring her back to Bialystok with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just a moment-- one day right after we got married, the Germans declare war to Russia. And next day, the Germans are already in Kaunas. Kaunas was around 30, 40 miles from the border, and unprotected, not defended by the Russians. And, and here we are with the Germans. And here starts, you know, we-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I could be killed, you know. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] The first days-- first days, it was for the Germans, you know, go against the Jews. There was-- Kaunas was surrounded with forts, 10 forts. Seven forts was the place where they would bring the Jews what they catch on the streets. And every soldier-- German soldier, who wanted to excel would come to kill the Jews, for the sake, for the glory of the Fuhrer. And they caught me. They bring me to the-- to the police precinct. And it is just an old [NON-ENGLISH]. And comes a, a, a probably a higher. And I told, told him, the only word what I knew how in Lithuanian, a doctor, [LITHUANIAN]. He looked at me, made a hand. And I-- he let me go.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was one of the encounters with death, very close. Was that a miracle?

speaker: interviewee
text: It is one of the miracles, yes. I know if, if I-- if he asked me something, I wouldn't be able to answer. I just say to him, [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: He believed you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Believed me. I was, you know, fine, you know, dressed nicely, et cetera.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go then afterwards?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to, to where I lived with my wife. We were living in the apartment of her relatives who fled to Russia.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was in Kaunas?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Kaunas.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now didn't you have a job--

speaker: interviewee
text: But we--

speaker: interviewer
text: --though, to get back to?

speaker: interviewee
text: But we have to go-- we have to go to the already announcement that the Jews had to go to this, this area. And on the-- until 15th of July, all Jews have to be concentrated in this area.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just tell me one thing though.

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: I thought you had a job. Didn't you have a job in Bialystok? Didn't you have to come back to Bialystok for that job?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bialystok was already occupied, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh-huh. So you didn't even have a job?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- it was in-- no communication. On the first day, they recognized I am a Jew. I didn't had to walk on the sidewalk. I have to walk-- next--

speaker: interviewer
text: On the street?

speaker: interviewee
text: --on the street.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because right away, you know, he started, you know, with persecution-- Jewish persecution.

speaker: interviewer
text: So tell me about Kaunas. Was that a ghetto that--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the ghetto was at the beginning, 33,000, 34,000 Jews. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] First was again, again, you know, I was working, started to work in an ambulance where the administration of the ghetto was located.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who ran the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jews from Kaunas. I-- the head was a even my senior, a doctor. A doctor was the head of the administration.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. If you want to know, I will get-- I can get all--

speaker: interviewer
text: It's OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: --the names, you know. But I--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the--

speaker: interviewer
text: This was--

speaker: interviewee
text: And the-- And the--

speaker: interviewer
text: This was 1941?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was 1941, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And here, you know, I enter. And I see that they are all [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] came to take 300 Jews to work in-- better in the libraries, and, and, and, and, and in I don't know where, you know-- we were-- but in better places--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: --in better places Hold on. Let me finish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, I'll tell you what. We have to continue because we won't be able to finish on this tape. We're going to continue on the next tape. This is the end of tape 2.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we are now on the third tape?

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to go into the third tape now.
